name: One-Liners
description: Rapier wit in a single sentence, from the sharpest tongues in comedy and letters.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: Please accept my resignation. I don't want to belong to any club that will accept people like me as a member.
quotes.notes: The wording of Groucho's resignation telegram as he recounts it in his autobiography; the shorter 'have me as a member' variant appears in The Groucho Letters (1967).

quotes.content: One morning I shot an elephant in my pajamas. How he got in my pajamas, I don't know.
quotes.notes: Captain Spaulding's non sequitur, among the most quoted gags in Marx Brothers cinema.

quotes.content: Why, a four-year-old child could understand this report. Run out and find me a four-year-old child—I can't make head or tail out of it.
quotes.notes: Rufus T. Firefly on a treasury report; frequently misquoted as 'a child of five.'

quotes.content: Either he's dead or my watch has stopped.
quotes.notes: Dr. Hackenbush, a horse doctor posing as a physician, takes a patient's pulse.

quotes.content: Whatever it is, I'm against it.
quotes.notes: The refrain of Professor Wagstaff's opening song at Huxley College (music Harry Ruby, lyrics Bert Kalmar).

quotes.content: I worked myself up from nothing to a state of extreme poverty.
quotes.notes: Groucho's self-deprecating boast aboard an ocean liner.

quotes.content: I could dance with you till the cows come home. On second thought, I'd rather dance with the cows till you came home.
quotes.notes: Firefly's backhanded pickup line to a young dancer.

quotes.content: Men seldom make passes at girls who wear glasses.
quotes.notes: The complete two-line poem 'News Item'; the near-sighted Parker removed her own glasses in company.

quotes.content: She ran the whole gamut of emotions from A to B.
quotes.notes: Parker's verdict on Katharine Hepburn in the play The Lake, spoken at intermission and printed by Time as 'run the gamut of human emotion from A to B.'

quotes.content: You can lead a horticulture, but you can't make her think.
quotes.source: You Might as Well Live: The Life and Times of Dorothy Parker (John Keats, 1970)
quotes.notes: Parker's pun answer during an Algonquin Round Table word game, recorded in Keats's biography.

quotes.content: What fresh hell can this be?
quotes.source: You Might as Well Live (John Keats, 1970)
quotes.notes: What Parker reportedly said whenever her doorbell rang, per friend Vincent Sheean; often misquoted as 'What fresh hell is this?'

quotes.content: He who can, does. He who cannot, teaches.
quotes.notes: From the aphorisms Shaw appended to his play.

quotes.content: The reasonable man adapts himself to the world; the unreasonable one persists in trying to adapt the world to himself. Therefore all progress depends on the unreasonable man.
quotes.notes: Shaw's paradox of progress, from the 'Reason' maxims.

quotes.content: The golden rule is that there are no golden rules.
quotes.notes: From the 'Golden Rule' section of the maxims.

quotes.content: Liberty means responsibility. That is why most men dread it.
quotes.notes: From the 'Liberty and Equality' maxims.

quotes.content: I never met a man I didn't like.
quotes.source: Remarks at Tremont Temple, Boston; reported in the Boston Globe (1930)
quotes.notes: Rogers used the line for years; its national spread followed this 1930 Boston remark.

quotes.content: The income tax has made more liars out of the American people than golf has.
quotes.notes: From his column 'Helping the Girls With Their Income Taxes.'

quotes.content: Everybody is ignorant, only on different subjects.
quotes.notes: From the essay 'Defending My Soup Plate Position.'

quotes.content: I don't make jokes. I just watch the government and report the facts.
quotes.source: Attributed; earliest prints via syndicated columns (1930s–40s)
quotes.notes: Exact-wording axis fails: Rogers told this repeatedly with varying phrasing and no single traceable original.

quotes.content: It ain't a fit night out for man or beast.
quotes.source: The Fatal Glass of Beer (film short, 1933)
quotes.notes: Fields repeats the line as fake snow hits his face each time he opens the cabin door.

quotes.content: Never give a sucker an even break.
quotes.notes: Fields's signature maxim, spoken to his daughter; later the title of his 1941 film.

quotes.content: Take my wife... please.
quotes.source: Signature line; title of his memoir Take My Wife, Please! (1973)
quotes.notes: Youngman's trademark one-liner, so bound to him that it titled his 1973 autobiography.

quotes.content: When I read about the evils of drinking, I gave up reading.
quotes.notes: Attribution axis fails: widely credited to Youngman but untraceable to a specific broadcast, album, or book.

quotes.content: Cleaning your house while your kids are still growing is like shoveling the walk before it stops snowing.
quotes.notes: The epigraph opening chapter one of her housekeeping parody.

quotes.content: I want my children to have all the things I couldn't afford. Then I want to move in with them.
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: a staple of her act with no traceable primary recording or first publication confirmed.

quotes.content: Motherhood is the second oldest profession in the world.
quotes.notes: The title thesis of her 1983 bestseller on parenting.

quotes.content: Never go to a doctor whose office plants have died.
quotes.notes: Advice from her column, printed in papers such as the Democrat and Chronicle on Jan. 10, 1975.
